citation: Funk JR, Cormier JM, Lessley DJ, Bailey AM, Sánchez EJ, Sherwood CP, Crandall JR, Arbogast KB, Myers BS, Mayer T, Sills AK. An Analysis of Guardian Cap Use and Changes in the Concussion Rate in National Football League Preseason Practices From 2018 to 2023. Am J Sports Med. 2025;1-8.
abstract: This cohort study examined the effect of the Guardian Cap NXT requirement on concussion incidence in NFL preseason practices from 2018 to 2023 (excluding 2020). Data from the NFL Injury Surveillance System were used to compare concussion rates before (2018, 2019, 2021) and after (2022, 2023) the Guardian Cap NXT mandate for offensive linemen, defensive linemen, tight ends, and linebackers. A total of 139 concussions occurred during the study period. Two Poisson regression models were run to assess overall concussion rates and helmet-shell-specific concussion rates while controlling for preseason, position, practice intensity, and running back/fullback status.
tldr: Requiring the Guardian Cap NXT was associated with a 54–62% reduction in NFL practice concussion rates for mandated positions, but the effect was no longer significant when only helmet-shell-involved concussions were analyzed, suggesting mechanisms beyond padding compression contributed to the reduction.
methods: Cohort study using NFL Injury Surveillance System data; concussion rates normalized to player practice exposures; univariate chi-square comparisons; two Poisson regression models (all concussions and helmet-shell concussions only); qualitative video review for impact source and helmet shell involvement; multiple imputation for missing shell impact data.
findings: • Guardian Cap NXT requirement associated with 54% reduction in concussion rate (univariate, P=.001) and 62% reduction in regression model (P=.004) for mandated positions. • No statistically significant association between Guardian Cap use and concussion rate when only helmet-shell concussions were analyzed (P=.122). • Concussions in GC group decreased from 23.3/year pre-requirement to 11.0/year post-requirement. • Non-GC group showed a 42% increase in concussions after the requirement (not attributable to Guardian Cap). • Padded practices had 3.97× higher concussion odds than unpadded practices. • Reduction was shared across all four required positions.
limitations: Small sample sizes in some subgroups; incremental rollout of Guardian Cap requirement; no data on Guardian Cap use by non-required players; inability to account for all factors affecting concussion risk including changes in practice style; video review unavailable for 18% of concussion cases; assumptions about universal compliance in required positions.
practicalImplications: Requiring padded helmet covers such as the Guardian Cap NXT for linemen, tight ends, and linebackers during practices may significantly reduce concussion incidence. Staff should note the benefit appears partially independent of shell padding compression (i.e., mass/inertia effects may contribute), and risk compensation behavior remains a theoretical concern that warrants monitoring.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to American football programs considering Guardian Cap NXT policies in preseason and regular-season contact practices. The mandate covering offensive/defensive linemen, tight ends, and linebackers reflects the highest-contact positions; programs at all levels may benefit from adopting similar policies during padded contact sessions.
rtp: This study does not directly address return-to-play protocols; however, reducing practice concussion incidence by 54–62% has downstream implications for athlete availability and recovery burden. Teams with lower practice concussion rates will face fewer RTP decisions and associated disruptions to development.

citation: Copeland A, Heinze KL. An examination of competitive rule evolution in the National Football League. Managing Sport and Leisure. 2024; DOI: 10.1080/23750472.2024.2369323.
abstract: This study examined how competitive rules evolve in professional sport using a qualitative document analysis of NFL rule books published between 1980 and 2020. The authors focused on player conduct rules—specifically unsportsmanlike conduct, unnecessary roughness, roughing the passer, chop blocking, crackback blocking, and clipping—to trace longitudinal trajectories of rule change. An inductive coding approach identified rule components (actions, actors, times, spaces, sanctions) and change types (expansion, elaboration, reduction, deletion). Findings were contextualized using NFL public communications to connect rule changes to institutional factors.
tldr: NFL player conduct rules generally expanded in scope and length from 1980 to 2020, driven by institutional pressures around player safety, officiating quality, and competitive fairness, offering a framework for understanding how sport rules evolve incrementally over time.
methods: Qualitative document analysis (QDA) of 35 NFL rule books (1980–2020); Microsoft Word 'compare documents' feature used to identify text changes at five-year intervals; inductive coding in NVivo to identify rule components and change types; analyst triangulation; trajectories constructed for six player conduct rules.
findings: • Rule changes generally expanded the set of prohibited actions and the number of actors protected or prohibited. • Rules applied to more game moments (times) and more field/corporeal spaces over time. • Sanctions for rule violations tended to increase in severity. • Rules followed an expansion-elaboration trajectory with a mid-period reduction phase before expanding again by 2020. • Rule word count increased substantially across the observation period. • Institutional pressures—player safety, officiating quality, league legitimacy—appeared to drive most changes.
limitations: Rule books from six seasons (1990, 1991, 2004, 2005, 2007, 2008) were missing and substituted with adjacent years; qualitative approach limits generalizability; only six rules were analyzed; focus on player conduct rules excludes scoring and other game mechanics rules; study does not measure outcomes of rule changes.
practicalImplications: Sport organizations and governing bodies can use this framework to track, anticipate, and justify rule changes by examining changes at the component level. Leaders should communicate rule changes in terms of their institutional rationale (safety, fairness, entertainment) to improve player, coach, and official buy-in.
athleteDev: While not directly a sport science study, understanding how NFL rules evolve—particularly safety-related rule changes (e.g., roughing the passer, crackback blocking)—helps coaching and sports science staff anticipate rule-driven changes to blocking, tackling, and contact techniques that affect player development and practice design in American football.
rtp: Not directly applicable to return-to-play protocols. However, rule evolution around player safety (e.g., defenseless player protections, targeting rules) influences the context in which head and contact injuries occur, which indirectly shapes RTP considerations at the professional level.

citation: Lundberg TR, Howatson G. Analgesic and anti-inflammatory drugs in sports: Implications for exercise performance and training adaptations. Scand J Med Sci Sports. 2018;1-11.
abstract: This narrative review synthesizes evidence on the effects of NSAIDs and paracetamol (acetaminophen) on exercise performance and long-term training adaptations in athletes. NSAIDs and paracetamol are widely consumed by athletes to manage pain and inflammation, yet both inhibit cyclooxygenase (COX) activity and have the potential to modulate tissue protein turnover. The review covers acute performance effects, exercise-induced protein synthesis, satellite cell activity, translational signaling, lipid mediator responses, training adaptations from chronic use, and safety and ethical considerations.
tldr: Paracetamol may acutely improve endurance and neuromuscular performance through increased pain tolerance, but both NSAIDs and paracetamol can attenuate anabolic responses to resistance exercise and may blunt long-term strength and hypertrophy gains in young individuals—a critical risk-benefit consideration for athletes using these drugs routinely.
methods: Narrative review; literature search covering acute performance trials (time trials, cycling, sprint, neuromuscular), acute cellular response studies (protein synthesis, satellite cells, translational signaling, lipid mediators), chronic training adaptation studies (resistance training in young and older adults), and safety/ethical literature; studies in both recreational and elite athletes reviewed.
findings: • Paracetamol improved cycling time-trial performance, heat exercise capacity, repeated sprint performance, and maximal voluntary contractions in several studies. • Both ibuprofen and paracetamol suppressed post-exercise muscle protein synthesis in young individuals via COX inhibition. • NSAIDs attenuated satellite cell proliferation response to resistance exercise. • High-dose ibuprofen (1200 mg/day) attenuated muscle hypertrophy and strength gains after 8 weeks of resistance training in young adults; low-dose ibuprofen showed no effect. • NSAIDs may positively influence training adaptations in older individuals. • No performance benefit of analgesics was shown for marathon running pain outcomes. • NSAIDs carry risks of GI complications, renal impairment, thrombocyte dysfunction, and increased mortality risk at higher doses.
limitations: Most acute adaptation studies used extreme eccentric protocols not representative of typical training; few studies in elite athletes; no studies examining paracetamol's effect on training adaptations in young individuals; lack of dose-response data; cross-sectional designs limit causal inference; potential ethical issues with analgesic use for competitive advantage.
practicalImplications: Athletes and support staff should conduct a careful risk-benefit analysis before routinely using NSAIDs or paracetamol during training. Routine NSAID use during resistance training may blunt hypertrophy and strength gains in younger athletes, while chronic use may mask pain signals and increase injury risk. A professional guidance and individualized approach is strongly advised.
athleteDev: Directly relevant to American football athlete development: frequent use of NSAIDs by football players (documented extensively at the FIFA World Cup and collegiate levels) during preseason and in-season resistance training may attenuate the anabolic adaptations critical for linemen and skill positions. Strength and conditioning staff should be aware that high-dose NSAID use during hypertrophy-focused training blocks could compromise gains.
rtp: Chronic analgesic use that dampens pain perception may cause athletes to return to play prematurely, increasing re-injury risk. NSAIDs' GI and cardiovascular risks are particularly relevant in collision sports where acute trauma is frequent. Staff should consider that NSAID-mediated blunting of the inflammatory repair response could delay true tissue healing despite pain resolution.

citation: O'Malley GR Jr, Sarwar SA, Posner KM, Polavarapu H, Adams D, Cassimatis ND, Munier S, Hundal JS, Patel NV. Analysis of Concussions Reported in American Professional Football Injury Reports in the 2019 through 2023 Regular Seasons. World Neurosurg. 2024; doi: 10.1016/j.wneu.2024.05.026.
abstract: This observational study examined trends in concussion diagnosis and return-to-play (RTP) across five NFL regular seasons (2019–2023) using publicly available NFL injury reports. The authors identified 664 of 692 (96%) officially recorded in-game concussions across the study period. Key outcomes included concussion rate per game, weeks missed following concussion, position-specific concussion frequency, and the impact of the Tua Tagovailoa injury and subsequent 'Tua Rule' protocol update on concussion reporting and RTP timelines.
tldr: NFL concussion rates remained largely stable across 2019–2023 with no significant change in weeks missed, though concussions per game increased after the Tua Tagovailoa incident and subsequent protocol changes; cornerbacks and wide receivers sustained the most concussions, and playoff teams had significantly shorter concussion RTP times.
methods: Observational study; NFL injury reports queried for 2019–2023 regular seasons; weeks missed calculated from NFL game participation logs; player position and team data extracted from NFL player profiles and Pro Football Reference; statistical analysis using R (tidyverse, skimr, FactoMineR packages); Z-scores for means/proportions comparisons; alpha=0.05; excluded concussions concurrent with other injuries, roster changes, or at season end.
findings: • 664 of 692 (96%) official in-game concussions identified across 2019–2023 (132, 125, 119, 141, 147 per season). • No significant difference in concussions per game across seasons (F=0.568, p=0.6836). • 31% of players returned without missing a game; 39% missed one game; 30% missed two or more games. • Cornerbacks led all positions with 119 concussions; wide receivers had 116; offensive linemen 94. • Playoff teams had significantly shorter concussion RTP than non-playoff teams (0.86 vs. 1.37 weeks, p=0.002). • Concussion rate per game increased after the Tagovailoa injury (0.521 vs. 0.429, p<0.01). • No difference in weeks missed before vs. after the Tua Rule implementation (1.51 vs. 1.31, p=0.24). • Players with second concussions in a season missed more games than for their first (2.68 vs. 1.00 weeks, p=0.000245).
limitations: Reliance on publicly available injury reports may undercount concussions not appearing on the weekly report; lack of access to NFL electronic medical records prevents severity assessment; psychosocial factors and motivational influences on RTP not assessable; concussion rate increase post-Tagovailoa may partially reflect improved detection rather than true incidence increase.
practicalImplications: NFL-level concussion surveillance data demonstrate that protocol changes (such as the Tua Rule adding ataxia as a mandatory removal criterion) increased detection rates without shortening RTP timelines, suggesting enhanced identification without over-restriction. Teams should monitor players with recurrent same-season concussions closely given significantly longer missed time for second events.
athleteDev: Cornerbacks and wide receivers face the highest NFL concussion burden—positions defined by high-speed open-field collisions. American football programs should consider position-specific concussion prevention strategies targeting skill players, including technique coaching, helmet optimization, and contact exposure monitoring during practice.
rtp: 31% of NFL players returned without missing a game following concussion, and mean weeks missed ranged 1.23–1.61 across seasons. Players sustaining a second concussion in the same season missed nearly three times as many weeks as for their first. Playoff teams demonstrated significantly faster RTP (0.86 weeks), which may reflect less severe concussion profiles or psychosocial motivation—a distinction with important clinical implications for RTP decision-making.

citation: Krill MK, Borchers JR, Hoffman JT, Krill ML, Hewett TE. Analysis of Football Injuries by Position Group in Division I College Football: A 5-Year Program Review. Clin J Sport Med. 2018;0:1-8.
abstract: This prospective, observational study evaluated injury characteristics across three position groups—line (offensive and defensive linemen), skill (defensive backs, wide receivers, running backs), and other (linebackers, tight ends, quarterbacks, special teams)—in a single major Division I collegiate football program over five fall regular seasons (2012–2016). A total of 200 injuries from 48,615 athlete exposures (AEs) were analyzed. Injury rates (IRs) per 1000 AEs and injury rate ratios (IRRs) were calculated across time of season, body part, type of injury, mechanism of injury, and type of exposure.
tldr: Skill position players had the highest overall injury rate—particularly in the preseason from noncontact mechanisms—while line and other position players sustained most injuries during the regular season from contact mechanisms; reducing preseason soft-tissue muscle strains in skill players and contact exposures for linemen represent the best opportunities to lower collegiate football injury rates.
methods: Prospective observational study at a single Division I program (2012–2016); NCAA Injury Surveillance System (ISS) reportable injury definition; injuries classified by time in season (preseason, first half, second half), body part, type (sprain, strain, fracture, concussion, etc.), mechanism (contact vs. noncontact), and exposure type (noncontact, full-pad, live contact); injury rates per 1000 AEs with 95% CIs; IRRs compared using Mid-P exact tests; weighted average AEs by position group calculated from full team roster.
findings: • Overall 5-year IR was 4.11 per 1000 AEs (95% CI 3.57–4.72). • Skill players had highest IR (7.56 per 1000 AEs) vs. line (4.26) and other (4.10). • Skill players had significantly higher IRR in preseason vs. line (IRR 1.75, P<0.05) and other (IRR 1.85, P<0.05). • Preseason IR was highest across all groups (5.47 per 1000 AEs). • Skill players sustained 50.5% of injuries from noncontact MOI vs. ~65–74% contact MOI for line and other. • Lower extremity injuries most common across all groups; skill players had significantly higher upper leg/thigh IRR. • Sprains, strains, and concussions were the three most common injury types across all groups. • No significant IRRs between position groups and type of exposure.
limitations: Single institution limits generalizability to other Division I programs; inexact AE counts by position group on a daily basis required weighted averages; inability to track weather, playing surface, or other environmental variables; skill position players may be underrepresented in AE calculations due to positional roster variability.
practicalImplications: Targeting preseason noncontact muscle strain reduction for skill position players (e.g., progressive loading, movement preparation protocols) represents a high-leverage injury prevention opportunity. Reducing contact exposures for linemen and other position players may have the greatest impact on concussions and contact mechanism injuries.
athleteDev: American football programs should differentiate injury prevention strategies by position group: skill players (DBs, WRs, RBs) require preseason emphasis on soft-tissue strain prevention through workload management and neuromuscular training, while linemen and linebackers benefit most from contact exposure reduction protocols during camp. The preseason period carries the highest injury risk across all positions.
rtp: The study did not specifically analyze return-to-play timelines, but concussion was the third most common injury type across all position groups (IR 0.84 per 1000 AEs for all combined), with the other position group (LBs, TEs, QBs) sustaining the highest concussion IR (1.00 per 1000 AEs). Position-specific concussion data can inform targeted monitoring and RTP protocols for the most at-risk groups.
